ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lamb sheekh kebab; 'kebab food product' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest broader class. Lamb-specific sheekh kebab is not individually enumerated in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (lamb meat, minced, cooked) is the closest defensible match. A dedicated sheekh kebab code does not exist in FoodEx2; spiced and skewer-roasted preparation is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for lamb sheekh kebab as a specific food entity as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Lamb Sheekh Kebab in Singapore consists of approximately 2 skewers, weighing around 120g, often served with mint chutney, sliced onions, and flatbread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Sheekh Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lamb Sheekh Kebab (cr_id G00010249). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb mince cooked data (NDB No. 17066) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on spiced minced meat preparation with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Sheekh Kebab in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the analogous category of spiced minced meat dishes with low carbohydrate content (5.24g/100g). The carbohydrate contribution is primarily from spices and onion binders; the protein and fat matrix further attenuates glycaemic response. GL per 120g serving = (30 × 5.24 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 1.9, rounded to 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 1080mg/100g — far exceeding the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Lamb Sheekh Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080mg/100g; if consumed, request reduced salt in preparation, avoid additional sauces or chutneys, and limit portion to no more than 60g per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24g/100g) and low GI support glycaemic stability, but high total fat (26.4g/100g) and high saturated fat (~11.2g/100g) require monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb Sheekh Kebab has a low carbohydrate content and low estimated GI, making it broadly compatible with a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan in moderate portions; however, the high total fat and saturated fat content should be monitored to avoid worsening insulin resistance, and sweet chutneys or dipping sauces should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.2g/100g), high sodium (1080mg/100g), and high cholesterol (85mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Lamb Sheekh Kebab only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and elevated cholesterol can raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; substituting with chicken or turkey sheekh kebab prepared with reduced salt is strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies RA inflammatory pathways; high sodium worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Lamb Sheekh Kebab as lamb is a primary dietary trigger for RA inflammation due to its high arachidonic acid content; the high sodium content further exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling, and substitution with chicken sheekh kebab with reduced salt is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Sheekh Kebab is a South Asian grilled meat dish made from minced lamb blended with aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, black pepper, cardamom, ginger, garlic, green chilli, and fresh coriander leaves. The spiced mince is moulded onto flat metal or bamboo skewers and roasted or grilled over high heat until cooked through with a lightly charred exterior. The texture is firm yet juicy, with a smoky, spiced flavour profile characteristic of tandoor or open-flame cooking. In Singapore, Lamb Sheekh Kebab is commonly served at Indian Muslim restaurants and hawker stalls, typically accompanied by mint chutney, sliced raw onions, lemon wedges, and flatbread such as roti or naan. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7g protein, 26.4g fat (of which approximately 11.2g is saturated), 5.24g carbohydrate, 2.85g sugar, and 1080mg sodium. The high sodium content makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The low carbohydrate content and low estimated glycaemic index (~30) make it broadly compatible with Type 2 Diabetes meal plans in moderate portions, though the high saturated fat warrants monitoring. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is typically consumed as a main course at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lamb sheekh kebab?
Is lamb seekh kebab good for diabetics?

Is lamb sheekh kebab gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Lamb Sheekh Kebab (cr_id G00010249) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked lamb mince data (SR Legacy NDB 17066) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry; this adaptation is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (11.2g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (10.8g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.5g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked ground lamb (NDB 17066) as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this dish. These values are adapted proportionally to the total fat of 26.4g/100g reported by HPB. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Lamb Sheekh Kebab preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact dish as of 2026. 3. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0g/100g per HPB source; this is plausible given the minimal vegetable content in the kebab mixture. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) based on the low carbohydrate content (5.24g/100g) and the protein-fat matrix of spiced minced meat; no direct GI measurement for Lamb Sheekh Kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 120g serving ≈ 2.0. 5. Halal status is assumed based on the dish being a standard offering at Indian Muslim establishments in Singapore; kosher status is not applicable. 6. FoodOn match is broader class 'kebab food product' (FOODON_03302051); no species-specific sheekh kebab term exists. FoodEx2 code A00GJ (lamb meat, minced, cooked) is the closest adapted match.
